evidence.text: No publication-year restriction (i.e., do not exclude by year).
Our search queries were executed in June 2023 and the found papers span a time period from 2011 to 2023.
From 2011 to 2023.
In case of multiple papers related to the same contribution, we reviewed the most recent paper.
Publications after the preparation of this manuscript (August 2024) will also not be included.
Fallback upper bound uses the SR's own arXiv publication date (2024-09-10).

normalization_notes: This is the most important correction in the whole folder. The current file disables time filtering entirely because it follows the source-faithful Stage 2 rule only.
For the lazy one-shot prefilter you described, that is too weak: the June 2023 search-execution date is a real time constraint on the candidate pool and therefore must be projected into time_policy.
The August 2024 manuscript horizon and the SR publication-date fallback are kept for completeness, but neither is binding once the June 2023 clause is included.

The explicit 'no publication-year restriction' is still preserved under source_faithful_screening_publication_policy, so the file now cleanly records both the paper-literal rule and your operational prefilter.

normalization_notes: This is the most important correction in the whole folder. The current file disables time filtering entirely because it follows the source-faithful Stage 2 rule only.
For the lazy one-shot prefilter you described, that is too weak: the June 2023 search-execution date is a real time constraint on the candidate pool and therefore must be projected into time_policy.
The August 2024 manuscript horizon and the SR publication-date fallback are kept for completeness, but neither is binding once the June 2023 clause is included.

The explicit 'no publication-year restriction' is still preserved under source_faithful_screening_publication_policy, so the file now cleanly records both the paper-literal rule and your operational prefilter.

evidence.text: Published from 2020 onward.
For arXiv papers, the citation count must meet year-specific thresholds (2020: 28, 2021: 22, 2022: 32, 2023: 90, 2024: 13, 2025: 1).
Active pre-review cutoff lower bound widened to 2010-01-01.
Fallback upper bound uses the SR's own arXiv publication date (2025-11-17).

normalization_notes: The active operational lower bound has been widened from 2020-01-01 to 2010-01-01.
The Stage 1 '2020 onward' clause is preserved for source-audit purposes but is no longer projected into the global pre-review cutoff.
Those citation thresholds are subset-specific and non-window-like, so they stay in time_clauses with project_to_time_policy=false.

evidence.text: No publication-year restriction (i.e., do not exclude by year).
Our search queries were executed in June 2023 and the found papers span a time period from 2011 to 2023.
From 2011 to 2023.
In case of multiple papers related to the same contribution, we reviewed the most recent paper.
Publications after the preparation of this manuscript (August 2024) will also not be included.
Fallback upper bound uses the SR's own arXiv publication date (2024-09-10).

normalization_notes: This is the most important correction in the whole folder. The current file disables time filtering entirely because it follows the source-faithful Stage 2 rule only.
For the lazy one-shot prefilter you described, that is too weak: the June 2023 search-execution date is a real time constraint on the candidate pool and therefore must be projected into time_policy.
The August 2024 manuscript horizon and the SR publication-date fallback are kept for completeness, but neither is binding once the June 2023 clause is included.

The explicit 'no publication-year restriction' is still preserved under source_faithful_screening_publication_policy, so the file now cleanly records both the paper-literal rule and your operational prefilter.

evidence.text: No publication-year restriction (i.e., do not exclude by year).
Our search queries were executed in June 2023 and the found papers span a time period from 2011 to 2023.
From 2011 to 2023.
In case of multiple papers related to the same contribution, we reviewed the most recent paper.
Publications after the preparation of this manuscript (August 2024) will also not be included.
Fallback upper bound uses the SR's own arXiv publication date (2024-09-10).

normalization_notes: This is the most important correction in the whole folder. The current file disables time filtering entirely because it follows the source-faithful Stage 2 rule only.
For the lazy one-shot prefilter you described, that is too weak: the June 2023 search-execution date is a real time constraint on the candidate pool and therefore must be projected into time_policy.
The August 2024 manuscript horizon and the SR publication-date fallback are kept for completeness, but neither is binding once the June 2023 clause is included.

The explicit 'no publication-year restriction' is still preserved under source_faithful_screening_publication_policy, so the file now cleanly records both the paper-literal rule and your operational prefilter.

evidence.text: Published from 2020 onward.
For arXiv papers, the citation count must meet year-specific thresholds (2020: 28, 2021: 22, 2022: 32, 2023: 90, 2024: 13, 2025: 1).
Active pre-review cutoff lower bound widened to 2010-01-01.
Fallback upper bound uses the SR's own arXiv publication date (2025-11-17).

normalization_notes: The active operational lower bound has been widened from 2020-01-01 to 2010-01-01.
The Stage 1 '2020 onward' clause is preserved for source-audit purposes but is no longer projected into the global pre-review cutoff.
Those citation thresholds are subset-specific and non-window-like, so they stay in time_clauses with project_to_time_policy=false.

evidence.text: No publication-year restriction (i.e., do not exclude by year).
Our search queries were executed in June 2023 and the found papers span a time period from 2011 to 2023.
From 2011 to 2023.
In case of multiple papers related to the same contribution, we reviewed the most recent paper.
Publications after the preparation of this manuscript (August 2024) will also not be included.
Fallback upper bound uses the SR's own arXiv publication date (2024-09-10).

normalization_notes: This is the most important correction in the whole folder. The current file disables time filtering entirely because it follows the source-faithful Stage 2 rule only.
For the lazy one-shot prefilter you described, that is too weak: the June 2023 search-execution date is a real time constraint on the candidate pool and therefore must be projected into time_policy.
The August 2024 manuscript horizon and the SR publication-date fallback are kept for completeness, but neither is binding once the June 2023 clause is included.

The explicit 'no publication-year restriction' is still preserved under source_faithful_screening_publication_policy, so the file now cleanly records both the paper-literal rule and your operational prefilter.

evidence.text: Published from 2020 onward.
For arXiv papers, the citation count must meet year-specific thresholds (2020: 28, 2021: 22, 2022: 32, 2023: 90, 2024: 13, 2025: 1).
Active pre-review cutoff lower bound widened to 2010-01-01.
Fallback upper bound uses the SR's own arXiv publication date (2025-11-17).

normalization_notes: The active operational lower bound has been widened from 2020-01-01 to 2010-01-01.
The Stage 1 '2020 onward' clause is preserved for source-audit purposes but is no longer projected into the global pre-review cutoff.
Those citation thresholds are subset-specific and non-window-like, so they stay in time_clauses with project_to_time_policy=false.

runs.combined_senior_reasoning:
Summary judgment: Include (5) — the paper presents a learned ordinal-ranking model for audio utterances and converts ratings to ordinal ranks, satisfying the preference-learning inclusion criterion.

Evidence by criterion (all quotes/paraphrases drawn from the provided full text):
1) Pertains to preference learning (ranking/A-B comparisons or equivalent): Supported. The paper explicitly frames the problem as an ordinal SER (speech emotion recognition) task and transforms K-rank ordinal classification into binary rank-comparison sub-tasks. Evidence: “we convert a rank label yi into a vector consisting of K−1 binary labels (y1_i, · · · , yK−1_i), where yk_i (k = 1, 2, · · · , K−1) indicates whether yi exceeds rank rk (i.e., yk_i = 1{yi > rk})” (Section 4.1). This is an explicit formulation that operationalizes ranking decisions per audio utterance and is a learned ranking-like objective rather than only numeric regression or purely evaluation.

2) Numeric ratings converted to rankings: Supported. The authors convert continuous valence ratings into discrete ordinal ranks for IEMOCAP: “we discretize the real-valued valence ratings ranging from 1 to 5 ... 1-Non-negative contains ratings in the range (3,5], 2-Somewhat Negative ... 3-Obviously Negative ...” (Section 5.1). This directly meets the criterion that numeric ratings can count when turned into rankings.

3) Applied within the audio domain: Supported. The paper uses recorded customer support calls and IEMOCAP speech data: “The database is created from recorded customer support calls in Chinese (8 kHz, mono).” and “we evaluate ... the IEMOCAP corpus (16 kHz, mono)” (Sections 3 and 5.1).

4) Learning component (not just evaluation): Supported. The paper trains a CORAL-based deep network (VGGish-CORAL) to predict ordinal ranks: “we propose to model the ordinal SER tasks under a ... CORAL based deep learning framework” and detailed training loss Eq. (2) and architecture (Sections 1, 4, 5.2). This is an actual learned model that optimizes ranking-consistent objectives.

5) Not a survey/review: Supported. The text describes novel method, experiments, datasets, and results (Introduction, Method, Experiments), i.e., an original research contribution.

Missing / potential counter-evidence (traceable):
- The work does not use explicit pairwise A/B preference annotations or pairwise comparison learning; instead it trains to predict absolute ordinal labels and reformulates ordinal labels into binary threshold tasks (Section 4.1). The inclusion criterion allows numeric ratings converted to rankings or ranking tasks broadly; pairwise comparisons are not strictly required.
- The paper does not include an RL training loop; the authors use supervised DNN training (Sections 4 and 5). The inclusion rules allow papers without explicit comparisons if they have an RL loop, but the converse (ranking via ordinal labels) is also acceptable per the stated inclusion rules.

Conclusion: The paper presents supervised ordinal (rank) learning on audio utterances, converts numeric ratings to discrete ranks, and trains a CORAL-based DNN to predict those ranks — satisfying the preference-learning inclusion definition. Therefore the paper should be included.

One-sentence justification: The paper implements learned ordinal (rank) prediction on audio utterances (via binary-threshold sub-tasks and converted ordinal ratings) and reports supervised results on speech datasets, meeting the stated preference-learning and audio-domain criteria.

runs.combined_senior_reasoning:
Summary of relevant criteria evidence from the full text (traceable quotes included):

1) Paper is applied in the audio domain and is not a survey/review (supported)
- Evidence: Title and abstract: "Ordinal learning for emotion recognition in customer service calls"; dataset description: "The database is created from recorded customer support calls in Chinese (8 kHz, mono)."
- Evidence: Not a survey: the paper describes an original method (VGGish-CORAL), experiments, and results (Sections 4–6).

2) Does the paper pertain to preference learning as defined (ranking or A/B comparison of two or more audio clips; numeric ratings count only when converted to rankings)? (not supported / missing)
- Evidence of ordinal labels but not pairwise preferences: "each utterance is labeled by 10 annotators on a 3-point scale: 1-Non-negative, 2-Somewhat Negative, and 3-Obviously Negative." (Section 3)
- Evidence that the method transforms ordinal labels into binary sub-tasks: "we convert a rank label yi into a vector consisting of K −1 binary labels (y1_i, · · · , yK−1_i), where yk_i (k = 1, 2, · · · , K −1) indicates whether yi exceeds rank rk (i.e., yk_i = 1{yi > rk})." (Section 4.1)
- Evidence the approach produces absolute rank predictions per utterance: "the final rank of an utterance can be predicted based on the results of a series of binary SER sub-tasks." (Conclusion)
- Explicit contrast with pairwise preference work: the paper itself contrasts prior pairwise preference learning: "They evaluated the emotional ranking between each pair of samples, but did not classify emotions directly into pre-set ranks described on an interval scale as our work requires." (end of Section 2). This indicates the present work does not perform pairwise/A-B comparisons.

3) Does the paper contain a reinforcement-learning (RL) training loop for an audio model (which would make it eligible even without explicit comparisons)? (not supported / missing)
- No mention of reinforcement learning, policy/value functions, rewards, or training loops in the full text. The training is standard supervised learning minimizing cross-entropy-like loss (Eq. 2) on per-utterance targets.

Conclusion with respect to inclusion criteria:
- Supported: (a) audio-domain application and (b) not a survey. These are satisfied by explicit statements in the full text.
- Not supported / missing: (a) explicit preference-learning as defined (pairwise or listwise ranking / A-B comparisons among audio clips); (b) an RL training loop.

Per the instructions to be conservative when required evidence is missing (instruction 4), and because the paper uses per-utterance ordinal labels and an ordinal-classification formulation (CORAL) rather than pairwise or listwise preference annotations or RL, I judge that the evidence for treating this as preference learning (in the required sense of ranking/A-B comparisons) is insufficient and therefore give the conservative rating.

One-sentence justification: The paper works with per-utterance ordinal labels and trains an ordinal-classification network (CORAL) but contains no evidence of pairwise/listwise preference annotations or any RL training loop, so it does not meet the stated preference-learning inclusion requirement as presented in the full text.
